Telstra has blamed the lack of a software update for a key time-keeping system for outage that caused nation-wide chaos last week, with its maintenance teams also unaware of a design change that affected how it would reset.

Ahead of an appearance by its chief executive, Vicki Brady, at a Senate inquiry over the mobile outage on Friday, Telstra provided a written submission that revealed the cause. In it, the company stated it didn’t lack redundancy in its network – but the redundancy also did not prevent the outage.

items.title: News live: Penny Wong says Australia ‘bitterly disappointed’ in Laos poisoning charges as ambassador called in

Bianca Jones and Holly Morton-Bowles died in November 2024 after inadvertently drinking alcohol laced with lethal methanol at Vang Vieng in Laos. Follow today’s news live.

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Pauline Hanson has been called “un-Australian” over her appearance on a podcast with far-right UK activist, Tommy Robinson.

The Greens senator Sarah Hanson-Young told ABC RN Hanson must return to Australia and apologise.

This is so appalling, and frankly, it’s unAustralian.

To go overseas to hang out with a criminal thug … to be laughing on his show about multiculturalism back here in Australia – which are our communities, Australian citizens, and the people who make this country great, Pauline Hanson is the most unAustralian politician in the parliament, and she should come home, face the music, and apologise.

I‘m not sure where Ms Hanson is getting her figures from, but they’ve never been provided to me as the minister for disability and the minister for the NDIS.

I suspect they don’t exist.

I’m loath to respond to a podcast between Ms Hanson and this convicted criminal, who frankly has been disowned by so many leading figures on the right.

The Lebanese foreign minister, Youssef Raggi, said Lebanon has made a decision to “end Hezbollah’s military presence” and that decisions on war and foreign policy are the “exclusive prerogative of the Lebanese state”.

The Lebanese government is pushing to disarm Hezbollah, one of the most heavily armed militias in the Middle East, and it has become a central component of the US-brokered talks between Lebanon and Israel.

Lebanon has made its choice: there will be no return to dual authority, and there is no longer any place for weapons outside the authority of the state or for decisions taken outside its constitutional institutions.

The decision to end Hezbollah’s military presence is a sovereign Lebanese decision. It preceded the Framework Agreement and paved the way for it, affirming that decisions on war and peace, as well as foreign policy, are now the exclusive prerogative of the Lebanese state.”

A former member of Morocco’s domestic intelligence service has helped to provide an unprecedented insight into how the north African state used hacking software – including Pegasus spyware – to target journalists, human rights defenders, French politicians and Spanish cabinet ministers and police officers.

Pegasus, which is manufactured by the Israel-based NSO Group, allows its operator to access everything on a target’s mobile phone, including emails, text messages and photographs. It can also activate the phone’s recorder and camera, turning it into a listening device.

items.title: Weather tracker: Typhoon leaves people stranded on rooftops in China
items.content_html:
Eleven reported dead as flooding also brings danger of snakes, while buildings collapse in Mumbai amid heavy rain

As the first typhoon to make landfall in China for the 2026 season, Maysak has caused devastating damage in southern and central regions. The Guangxi region received intense downpours of up to 280mm in 12 hours, causing rivers to swell and dam walls to break. By Monday morning, flooding across the city of Nanning and surrounding areas had resulted in many people being stranded on rooftops.

Flood waters pose additional threats in China because of the presence of wild and farmed snakes. On Thursday local media reported that hundreds of snakes, including cobras, had escaped from flooded breeding farms. Typhoon Maysak also aided the development of two destructive tornados that swept across central China later on Monday evening. This occurred when warm air from the south, brought up by Typhoon Maysak, collided with cold air in the north.

items.title: Trump offers Iran an economic lifeline — with strings
items.content_html: Iran has seen a speedy rebound in oil exports after the US-Iran deal granted immediate waivers on US sanctions. Relief on frozen assets and a $300 billion reconstruction fund now hinge on Tehran meeting key conditions.

items.title: Why Austrian author Ingeborg Bachmann remains a literary icon
items.content_html: A new documentary featuring Sandra Hüller celebrates the Austrian poet and author who was a literary star of her era. She would have turned 100 this year.

items.title: Experts axed by Trump keep climate information flowing
items.content_html: Fired US federal workers have revived a defunct climate website — pushing back as the Trump administration escalates cuts to publicly funded science and research.

items.title: Europe is spending billions to rearm, so why the delays?
items.content_html: European countries have been trying to scale up their military capabilities, but their current defense production and procurement model is not fit for purpose, say experts.

items.title: More than a unibrow: The enduring appeal of Frida Kahlo
items.content_html: She shunned feminine beauty ideals, was bisexual and a political radical, all while being brutally honest in her art. A major Frida Kahlo exhibition opens at London's Tate Modern.

items.title: 75 years of Germany's Goethe-Institut
items.content_html: For 75 years, the Goethe-Institut has stood for cultural exchange, education and partnerships around the globe — even under challenging political conditions.

items.title: The man who knew, but didn't say: Alan Greenspan (1926-2026)
items.content_html: Alan Greenspan’s reputation as head of the US Federal Reserve was legendary — not least because of his reputation for convoluted statements. However, the 2008/9 financial crisis took a toll on his standing.

items.title: How TikTok-style videos keep human brains hooked on content
items.content_html: TikTok, Instagram and YouTube clips are designed to overwhelm the brain's pleasure circuitry and keep people watching. Researchers say short-form videos may only be the start when it comes to harvesting human attention.

items.title: France bans alcohol during Fete de la Musique amid heat wave
items.content_html: France is taking additional measures as millions of people gear up for Fete de la Musique under punishing temperatures.

items.title: EU ban on BPA chemical in food packaging takes effect
items.content_html: From July 2026 onwards, BPA will no longer be permitted in EU food packaging. The industrial chemical is known to interfere with the human hormonal system.

items.title: What to expect from the strongest El Nino in over a century
items.content_html: Warnings of a mighty El Nino weather pattern this year could bring drought, flooding and extreme heat. Can we prepare for the worst of it?

items.title: How cities are adapting to a warming world
items.content_html: As heat waves grow more frequent and fierce, cities worldwide are racing to cool streets and reshape urban life.

items.title: Can Europe build its own AI heavyweights to rival the US?
items.content_html: With AI dominated by US firms, Europe is pushing for more independence. New partnerships, investments and startups aim to boost the continent's digital sovereignty, but challenges around funding and regulation remain.

items.title: Women tackling coal dust and other eco wins this week
items.content_html: Indian women fight back against toxic coal dust, the US hits a solar milestone, and there's good news for soil in Kenya and cars worldwide.

items.title: New exhibitions for Anish Kapoor: Art on the edge
items.content_html: His art challenges our perception: Forms vanish and perspectives shift. Anish Kapoor leads the viewer to the edge of the abyss — and sometimes, even beyond. Two new exhibitions revisit his work.

items.title: US curbs Anthropic AI access, raising global concerns
items.content_html: The Trump administration has restricted foreign access to Anthropic's latest AI models over security fears, forcing a global shutdown. Could the row hurt the Claude-maker's IPO plans and set a far-reaching precedent?

items.title: How a feel-good German song is taking over social media
items.content_html: Being encouraging and building confidence is what the German song "Gut genug" ("Good Enough") is all about. A meme is now bringing the track and its message to millions of people around the world.

items.title: Berlin summit brings out private equity protesters
items.content_html: Activists gathered to protest the SuperReturn private equity summit in Berlin, saying the industry drives inequality, job cuts and rising costs as investors representing trillions of Euros gather to seek high returns.
